questions.question_text: When must a broker provide written disclosure of their brokerage relationship to a party?
distractors: Only at the time of contract signing
Within 48 hours of first contact
Before the closing date only
questions.explanation: NM requires written disclosure of existing brokerage relationships at the earliest practicable time — not waiting until contract signing or closing.

questions.question_text: A broker is considering purchasing a property that is listed by their own brokerage. The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their personal interest in writing
distractors: Withdraw from the transaction entirely
Only disclose if they are the listing agent
Disclose verbally to the seller at closing
questions.explanation: Brokers must provide written disclosure of any personal interest in a transaction. Buying a property through your own brokerage requires disclosure but doesn't necessarily require withdrawal.

questions.question_text: A buyer's broker discovers they are related to the seller. What must the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Provide written disclosure of the relationship
distractors: Immediately withdraw from the transaction
Continue without disclosure if no conflict exists
Disclose only if asked by the buyer
questions.explanation: A familial relationship with the other party is a personal interest that must be disclosed in writing at the earliest practicable time. The parties can then decide how to proceed.

questions.question_text: A broker represents a seller and also has a buyer interested in the same property. Before proceeding, the broker must disclose:
questions.correct_answer: The existing brokerage relationship in writing
distractors: Nothing until an offer is submitted
The relationship verbally during the showing
Only the commission structure to both parties
questions.explanation: The broker must provide written disclosure of existing brokerage relationships to all parties. This allows informed decision-making about dual agency or other arrangements.

questions.question_text: Which form of disclosure satisfies NM requirements for brokerage relationship disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: Written disclosure provided to the party
distractors: A verbal explanation during the first meeting
A posted notice in the brokerage office
An email sent after the transaction closes
questions.explanation: NM requires written disclosure of brokerage relationships. Verbal explanations, office postings, and post-closing emails do not satisfy the written disclosure requirement.

questions.question_text: A listing broker learns that the property has a history of flooding in the basement. The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this adverse material fact in writing
distractors: Keep the information confidential for the seller
Disclose only if the buyer asks specifically
Report the flooding to the county assessor
questions.explanation: Brokers must provide written disclosure of known adverse material facts to all parties. A flooding history is a material defect that affects the property's value and desirability.

questions.question_text: The duty to disclose adverse material facts applies to:
distractors: Only the broker's client
Only unrepresented parties
Only buyers with written agreements
questions.explanation: The duty to disclose known adverse material facts applies to all parties, not just clients. This protects both buyers and sellers regardless of representation status.

questions.question_text: A seller's broker knows the neighboring property will be developed into a landfill. Must the broker disclose this?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, it is an adverse material fact
distractors: No, the broker only discloses property defects
Only if the buyer's broker asks about it
No, off-site conditions are never disclosable
questions.explanation: Adverse material facts include environmental issues and material circumstances beyond just physical property defects. A planned neighboring landfill materially affects the property.

questions.question_text: A broker representing a seller suspects but is unsure about a roof leak. The broker's obligation is to:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the suspected condition to the buyer
distractors: Say nothing until the leak is confirmed
Have the roof repaired before showing
Tell the buyer to hire their own inspector
questions.explanation: The duty covers adverse material facts known to the broker. If the broker has knowledge of a suspected defect, disclosure is appropriate. Encouraging inspection is also good practice.

questions.question_text: How must a broker communicate adverse material facts under NM law?
questions.correct_answer: In writing to the affected parties
distractors: Verbally during a property showing
Through the other party's broker only
By posting a notice at the property
questions.explanation: NM requires written disclosure of adverse material facts. Verbal communication alone does not satisfy the requirement, even if the information is accurate.

questions.question_text: A seller tells their listing broker they will accept $20,000 less than the asking price. This information is:
distractors: Required to be shared with interested buyers
Disclosed only to the buyer's broker
Posted on the MLS as a price reduction
questions.explanation: A client's bargaining position — including the lowest acceptable price — is confidential information. The broker cannot disclose this to buyers or their agents.

questions.question_text: A buyer tells their broker they are highly motivated because of a job relocation deadline. The broker shares this with the listing agent to speed up negotiations. This is:
distractors: Good faith negotiation practice
Required to facilitate the transaction
Acceptable if the buyer doesn't object
questions.explanation: A client's motivations for buying are confidential. Sharing the buyer's urgency gives the seller leverage, harming the buyer's negotiating position.

questions.question_text: After a brokerage relationship ends, a broker's confidentiality obligation:
distractors: Ends immediately upon termination
Expires after one year
Transfers to the new broker
questions.explanation: In NM, confidentiality survives termination of the brokerage relationship. Former clients' confidential information must remain protected indefinitely.

questions.question_text: A former client's new broker asks for information about the client's financial condition from a previous transaction. The original broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Decline unless the client gives written consent
distractors: Share freely since the relationship ended
Provide the information to help the new broker
Report the request to NMREC
questions.explanation: Confidential information cannot be disclosed without the client's written consent, even after the relationship has ended. Financial condition is explicitly listed as confidential.

questions.question_text: Which type of information is specifically identified as confidential in NM brokerage relationships?
distractors: The property's listing price
The closing date of the transaction
The name of the other party
questions.explanation: NM specifically identifies the client's bargaining position, motivations for buying/selling, and financial condition as confidential information protected within brokerage relationships.
